topics.title: Introduction to control systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT501 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to control systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ­¤ōś Introduction to Control Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Control System** is an interconnection of components and subsystems forming a system configuration that will provide a desired system response. The fundamental purpose of a control system is to **regulate, direct, or command** the behavior of another system or process using feedback loops in order to achieve a desired output (reference value) despite the presence of disturbances, parameter variations, and uncertainties.

Mathematically, a control system can be represented as a mapping:

$$
C: \mathbb{I} \rightarrow \mathbb{O}
$$

where $\mathbb{I}$ is the input (reference/command) signal space and $\mathbb{O}$ is the output (controlled) signal space. In the Laplace domain, this is characterized by the **Transfer Function** $G(s) = Y(s)/R(s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The student must be able to **define, classify, and distinguish** between various types of control systems, and **explain the role and effects of feedback** in dynamic systems. This is a foundational module ŌĆö almost every later module references terminology established here.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and want to maintain a constant speed of **80 km/h**.

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Control System Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Your eyes | See the speedometer | **Sensor / Measuring element** |
| Your brain | Compare 80 vs current | **Comparator / Error detector** |
| Your foot | Press/release accelerator | **Controller / Actuator** |
| The car | Moves faster/slower | **Plant / Process** |
| The road / wind | Slopes, headwind | **Disturbances** |

You constantly **measure**, **compare**, and **correct**. This is exactly what a **closed-loop (feedback) control system** does. If, instead, you had a **cruise control set once and never looked at the speedometer**, that would be an **open-loop** system ŌĆö it trusts the input blindly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The presence of a **feedback path** that continuously informs the controller about the actual output is the single most important design choice in any control system.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Output (controlled variable)** $c(t)$ ŌĆö the quantity being regulated (e.g., temperature in ┬░C, voltage in V, speed in rad/s).
- **Reference input (set point)** $r(t)$ ŌĆö the desired value.
- **Actuating / error signal** $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$ ŌĆö drives the controller.
- **Plant** $G_p(s)$ ŌĆö the system being controlled.
- **Disturbance** $d(t)$ ŌĆö unwanted inputs (e.g., load torque, wind, noise). All disturbances are modeled with standard **units matching the output** (e.g., **N┬Ęm, V, ┬░C**).

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exam answers, always **label units** on the axes of any block diagram you draw. Examiners award marks specifically for correctly indicating signal types and their physical dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ­¤ö¼ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Control Systems

Control systems are classified along **multiple axes** in the KTU 2024 syllabus. The classification tree is structured below:

### 2.1.1 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Systems

| Feature | Open-Loop System | Closed-Loop (Feedback) System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback path | **Absent** | **Present** |
| Error correction | Cannot self-correct | Continuously corrects via $e(t)$ |
| Sensitivity to disturbances | **High** | **Low** (reduced by feedback) |
| Accuracy | Lower | Higher |
| Stability | Always stable (if components stable) | May become unstable ŌĆö major design concern |
| Cost & complexity | Low | High |
| Bandwidth | Wide | Narrow (sacrificed for stability) |
| Example | Toaster, washing machine (timer-based), traffic signal | Cruise control, AC thermostat, voltage regulator |

The **transfer functions** are:

- **Open-loop:** $C(s) = G(s)\,R(s)$
- **Closed-loop (unity feedback):**

$$
C(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} R(s)
$$

where $H(s)$ is the **feedback element** transfer function.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **unity feedback** system ($H(s) = 1$), the closed-loop transfer function simplifies to:

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

---

### 2.1.2 Linear vs. Non-Linear Systems

| Property | Linear System | Non-Linear System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Superposition | Holds ($f(ax_1 + bx_2) = af(x_1) + bf(x_2)$) | Fails |
| Governing equation | Linear ODE with constant coefficients | Contains products/powers of variables (e.g., $x^2$, $\sin x$, $x\,\dot{x}$) |
| Analysis tools | Laplace transform, Bode, Root Locus | Phase-plane, describing functions, Lyapunov |
| KTU treatment | Primary focus | Treated via linearization around operating point |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linearization Principle (KTU high-yield):** A non-linear system $f(x)$ is **linearized** about an operating point $x_0$ by retaining only the first-order term of its Taylor series:

$$
f(x) \approx f(x_0) + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{x_0} (x - x_0)
$$

---

### 2.1.3 Time-Invariant vs. Time-Varying Systems

- **Time-Invariant (LTI):** System parameters do **not change with time** ŌĆö coefficients of the governing ODE are constants.
- **Time-Varying (LTV):** Parameters change with time, e.g., a rocket losing mass as fuel burns.

### 2.1.4 Continuous-Time vs. Discrete-Time Systems

| Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time |
| :--- | :--- |
| Signals defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Signals defined at sampling instants $t = kT$ |
| Described by ODEs, Laplace transform | Described by difference equations, Z-transform |
| Example: analog circuit, DC motor | Example: digital controller, computer control |

### 2.1.5 SISO vs. MIMO Systems

- **SISO (Single-Input Single-Output):** One input, one output ŌĆö e.g., a single DC motor speed control.
- **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output):** Multiple inputs, multiple outputs ŌĆö e.g., an aircraft (roll, pitch, yaw each controlled simultaneously).

### 2.1.6 Servo Systems vs. Regulatory Systems (Process Control)

- **Servo (Tracking) system:** Input $r(t)$ varies with time; the output must **track** the input. Example: radar antenna, robotic arm.
- **Regulatory (Automatic) system:** Input $r(t)$ is **constant**; the goal is to maintain the output at the setpoint despite disturbances. Example: room thermostat, voltage regulator.

---

## 2.2 Effects of Negative Feedback (The "Why" of Feedback)

The four classical benefits of negative feedback, all **derivable for the KTU exam**, are:

| # | Effect | Mathematical Insight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Reduces sensitivity to parameter variations** | $S_G^T = \dfrac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$ |
| 2 | **Reduces steady-state error** | Error constant (e.g., $K_p, K_v$) increases by $(1+GH)$ |
| 3 | **Attenuates disturbance signals** | Disturbance transfer function scaled by $\dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ |
| 4 | **Improves transient response (bandwidth)** | Closed-loop bandwidth $\approx$ open-loop gain crossover |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Price of Feedback:** Feedback **reduces overall gain** by a factor of $(1+GH)$ and **may introduce instability** if not designed carefully. This trade-off is the central theme of classical control theory.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Notes / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Open-loop TF | $C(s) = G(s)\,R(s)$ | $G(s)$ is forward-path TF |
| 2 | Closed-loop TF (unity) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | $H(s)=1$ |
| 3 | Closed-loop TF (general) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | $H(s)$ feedback TF |
| 4 | Error signal | $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$ | $E(s) = R(s) / [1+G(s)H(s)]$ |
| 5 | Actuating signal | $E_a(s) = H(s)\,E(s)$ | For non-unity feedback |
| 6 | Sensitivity function | $S(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | $|S(j\omega)|$ small ŌćÆ robust |
| 7 | Complementary sensitivity | $T(s) + S(s) = 1$ | Always true |
| 8 | Disturbance rejection (at output) | $\dfrac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ | $D(s)$ enters at plant input |
| 9 | Linearization | $\Delta y \approx \left.\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x}\right|_{x_0} \Delta x$ | First-order Taylor approximation |
| 10 | Superposition | $f(ax_1 + bx_2) = af(x_1)+bf(x_2)$ | Linearity test |

> [!NOTE]
> **All transfer functions are dimensionless ratios of like quantities**, unless otherwise stated. Physical units of the output equal the units of the input multiplied by the units of $G(s)$ (e.g., $G(s)$ may have units of **rad/s per V** for a motor).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# ­¤¦« Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function

**Given:** A standard negative-feedback control system with forward transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback transfer function $H(s)$.

**Find:** The overall transfer function $T(s) = C(s) / R(s)$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify signals at the comparator

The error / actuating signal is the difference between the reference and the fed-back output:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - B(s)
$$

where $B(s) = H(s)\,C(s)$ is the **feedback signal** at the comparator.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Write the forward-path equation

The plant input equals the error signal, and the output is the plant input times the forward TF:

$$
C(s) = G(s)\,E(s)
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute Step 1's expression for $E(s)$ into Step 2

$$
C(s) = G(s)\left[\,R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)\,\right]
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Expand and collect $C(s)$ terms

$$
C(s) = G(s)\,R(s) - G(s)\,H(s)\,C(s)
$$

$$
C(s) + G(s)\,H(s)\,C(s) = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Factor $C(s)$ on the left side

$$
C(s)\left[\,1 + G(s)\,H(s)\,\right] = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for $C(s)/R(s)$

$$
\boxed{\;T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)\,H(s)}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization trick:** "Forward over one plus loop gain." The **loop gain** is the product of all elements around the closed loop: $L(s) = G(s)H(s)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Feedback on Sensitivity

**Given:** A change $\Delta G$ in the forward-path TF $G$. Find the relative change in the closed-loop TF $T$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Write the closed-loop TF

$$
T = \frac{G}{1 + GH}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the partial derivative $\partial T / \partial G$

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial G} = \frac{(1 + GH) - G \cdot H}{(1 + GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Form the sensitivity $S_G^T$

$$
S_G^T = \frac{\partial T / T}{\partial G / G} = \frac{G}{T} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial G}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $T$ and $\partial T / \partial G$

$$
S_G^T = \frac{G}{\dfrac{G}{1+GH}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} = (1 + GH) \cdot \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Final result

$$
\boxed{\;S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}\;}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The sensitivity of the closed-loop system to variations in $G$ is reduced by a factor of $1 / (1+GH)$ compared to the open-loop system. The higher the loop gain, the more robust the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $G(s)H(s) \gg 1$ at a given frequency, then $S_G^T \approx 1/(GH) \to 0$, meaning the closed-loop behavior becomes **almost independent of the plant's exact parameters** ŌĆö a cornerstone of robust control.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Behavior (Numerical Comparison)

Let $G(s) = 10 / (s+1)$ and $H(s) = 1$.

| Quantity | Open-Loop Value | Closed-Loop Value | Ratio (CL / OL) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| DC gain $\vert T(0) \vert$ | $10$ | $\dfrac{10}{1+10} = 0.909$ | $1 / 11 \approx 0.091$ |
| Sensitivity to $G$ | $1$ | $1 / 11$ | $0.091$ |
| Steady-state error to unit step | $1/10 = 0.1$ | $1/11 \approx 0.091$ | reduced |
| Bandwidth (rad/s) | $1$ | $\approx 11$ | increased $\times 11$ |
| Order of system | $1^{\text{st}}$ | $1^{\text{st}}$ | unchanged (no extra dynamics added) |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **fundamental trade-off**: gain is reduced from 10 to 0.909, but **bandwidth increases** ŌĆö a faster, more responsive system at the cost of amplification. This is the price of feedback that KTU examiners expect students to articulate.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example ŌĆö Linearization of a Non-Linear Plant

**Problem:** A pendulum has the non-linear equation $\ddot{\theta} + \sin(\theta) = u(t)$. Linearize about the operating point $\theta_0 = 0$ rad.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the non-linear function $f(\theta) = \sin(\theta)$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate $f$ and its derivative at the operating point

$$
\sin(0) = 0, \qquad \left.\frac{d(\sin\theta)}{d\theta}\right|_{\theta=0} = \cos(0) = 1
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the first-order Taylor expansion

$$
\sin(\theta) \approx \sin(0) + \cos(0)\cdot(\theta - 0) = \theta
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute back into the ODE

$$
\ddot{\theta} + \theta = u(t)
$$

This is the **linearized** governing equation, valid for small angles ($|\theta| \ll 1$ rad).

> [!TIP]
> In KTU module-end exams, you may be asked: *"Justify why linearization is valid only for small perturbations about the equilibrium."* The answer: higher-order Taylor terms ($O(\theta^3), O(\theta^5), \ldots$) are **neglected** ŌĆö they remain small only when $\theta$ is small.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ref[R s] -- r(t) --> sum1((+))
    sum1 -- e(t) --> ctrl[Controller Gc]
    ctrl -- u(t) --> plant[Plant Gp]
    plant -- c(t) --> out[Output Y s]
    plant -- disturbance path --> sumDist((+))
    sumDist --> out
    out -- feedback signal --> fb[Sensor H s]
    fb -- b(t) --> sum1
    sum1 -. negative sign .- sum1
```

**Signal flow in words:**

1. **Reference input** $r(t)$ enters the summing junction.
2. The summing junction subtracts the **feedback signal** $b(t)$ to form the **error** $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$.
3. The **controller** $G_c(s)$ processes $e(t)$ to produce control signal $u(t)$.
4. The **plant** $G_p(s)$ responds, producing output $c(t)$.
5. The **sensor** $H(s)$ measures $c(t)$ and feeds it back to the summing junction.
6. **Disturbance** $d(t)$ can enter at the plant input or output (shown at plant input here).

---

## 4.2 Comparison: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Block Diagrams

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OL["OPEN LOOP SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        rOL[Reference R s] -- r(t) --> cOL[Controller Gc]
        cOL -- u(t) --> pOL[Plant Gp]
        pOL -- c(t) --> yOL[Output C s]
    end
    subgraph CL["CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        rCL[Reference R s] -- r(t) --> sumCL((+))
        sumCL -- e(t) --> cCL[Controller Gc]
        cCL -- u(t) --> pCL[Plant Gp]
        pCL -- c(t) --> yCL[Output C s]
        yCL -- measured --> fbCL[Sensor H s]
        fbCL -- b(t) --> sumCL
    end
```

**Key visual difference:** The closed-loop system contains an **extra backward arrow** from output to summing junction ŌĆö that single arrow is what makes the system intelligent, robust, and self-correcting.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Signal Flow in a Real System (DC Motor Speed Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp[Setpoint RPM] --> cmp((+))
    cmp -- error e --> amp[PWM Amplifier]
    amp -- voltage v --> mot[DC Motor Plant]
    mot -- speed omega --> load[Mechanical Load]
    load -- actual omega --> tacho[Tachogenerator H s]
    tacho -- b voltage --> cmp
    wind[Disturbance Wind Load] -.-> mot
    torque[Load Torque Disturbance] -.-> load
```

**Reading guide for KTU answers:**

- **Setpoint** ŌåÆ command signal from user/dashboard.
- **Tachogenerator** = sensor; converts $\omega$ (rad/s) to a voltage (V) ŌĆö its gain $K_t$ has units of **V┬Ęs/rad**.
- **PWM Amplifier** = controller/actuator with gain $K_a$ (**V/V**).
- **DC Motor** = plant, with electrical time constant $\tau_e$ and mechanical time constant $\tau_m$.
- **Disturbances** (wind, friction, load) are shown as **dotted arrows** to distinguish them from the main signal flow.

---

## 4.4 Classification Flowchart of Control Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cs[Control System] --> A{Based on Feedback}
    A -- No --> OL[Open Loop]
    A -- Yes --> CL[Closed Loop]
    cs --> B{Based on Linearity}
    B -- Linear --> LIN[Linear]
    B -- Nonlinear --> NL[Nonlinear]
    cs --> C{Based on Time}
    C -- Continuous --> CT[Continuous Time]
    C -- Discrete --> DT[Discrete Time]
    cs --> D{Based on Inputs Outputs}
    D -- 1x1 --> SISO[SISO]
    D -- NxM --> MIMO[MIMO]
    cs --> E{Based on Application}
    E -- Tracking --> SV[Servo System]
    E -- Constant setpoint --> RG[Regulatory System]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# ­¤ōØ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
*Define a control system. Distinguish clearly between open-loop and closed-loop control systems with one real-world example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A control system is an arrangement of physical components organized to command, direct, or regulate itself or another system to achieve a desired output.

| Aspect | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Self-correction | Not possible | Possible via error signal |
| Disturbance rejection | Poor | Good |
| Example | Automatic washing machine (timer-based) | Room air conditioner with thermostat |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often give *two examples of closed-loop systems* instead of one open and one closed. Marks are split: **1 mark for definition**, **2 marks for the comparison table**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*List the four main effects of negative feedback in a control system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four effects of negative feedback are:
1. **Reduces sensitivity** of the system to parameter variations.
2. **Reduces steady-state error** to reference inputs.
3. **Attenuates the effect of disturbances** on system output.
4. **Improves transient response and bandwidth** of the system.

(One brief supporting line per point: e.g., "Sensitivity is reduced by a factor of $1/(1+GH)$.")

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do **not** list *"increases accuracy"* and *"reduces error"* as two separate points ŌĆö examiners treat them as one effect. Also do not forget the **drawback** of feedback (loss of gain, possible instability); mentioning it shows conceptual depth.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the fundamental components of a closed-loop control system. Define the terms: reference input, error signal, plant, feedback element, and controlled output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function $C(s)/R(s)$ of a negative feedback system having forward-path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback transfer function $H(s)$. Show that for $GH \gg 1$, the closed-loop TF becomes independent of $G$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rnode[R s] -- r(t) --> sump((+))
    sump -- e(t) --> Gc[Gc s]
    Gc -- u(t) --> Gp[Gp s]
    Gp -- c(t) --> Cnode[C s]
    Cnode --> Hnode[H s]
    Hnode -- b(t) --> sump
```

**Definitions (1 mark each, with the diagram carrying 2 marks):**

- **Reference input $r(t)$:** The desired value of the output, set by the user.
- **Error / actuating signal $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$:** The difference between the desired and actual values, which drives the controller.
- **Plant $G_p(s)$:** The physical system or process being controlled (e.g., motor, furnace, robot arm).
- **Feedback element $H(s)$:** The sensor or measuring device that monitors the output and sends it back.
- **Controlled output $c(t)$:** The actual variable being regulated (e.g., temperature, speed, position).

> [!TIP]
> Draw the diagram on the **left side of the page** and the definitions on the **right** ŌĆö KTU examiners value neatness and labeling.

---

#### **Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Derivation** (already shown in ┬¦3.1, repeated here for completeness):

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)
$$

$$
C(s) = G(s)\,E(s) = G(s)\,[R(s) - H(s)\,C(s)]
$$

$$
C(s)\,[1 + G(s)H(s)] = G(s)\,R(s)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)\,H(s)}\;}
$$

**Independence of $G$ when $GH \gg 1$:**

$$
\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} \bigg|_{GH \to \infty} = \frac{G(s)}{G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{H(s)}
$$

This shows that when the **loop gain** $GH$ is very large, the closed-loop behavior depends **only on the feedback element $H(s)$** ŌĆö not on the plant $G(s)$. This is the principle behind high-gain feedback and is fundamental to robust control design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall ŌĆö A common 2-mark loss:** Students forget to mention that "$GH \gg 1$" must hold in the **frequency range of interest**, not just at DC. Also, do not write "$GH = \infty$" ŌĆö the loop gain is always finite in practice.

---

### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare linear and non-linear control systems. Explain the principle of linearization of a non-linear system around an operating point with one example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unity feedback system has $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s+5}$. Compute (i) the closed-loop transfer function, (ii) the steady-state error for a unit step input, and (iii) the sensitivity of the closed-loop TF to a variation in the plant gain $G$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Property | Linear | Non-Linear |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Superposition | Holds | Fails |
| Analysis | Laplace, frequency response | Phase plane, Lyapunov, describing functions |
| Behavior | Predictable, unique equilibrium | Limit cycles, chaos, multiple equilibria |

**Linearization principle (3 marks):**

A non-linear function $f(x)$ is linearized about an operating point $x_0$ by:

$$
f(x) \approx f(x_0) + \left.\frac{df}{dx}\right|_{x_0} (x - x_0)
$$

**Example (1 mark):** For a pendulum with $\ddot{\theta} + \sin\theta = u(t)$, linearization about $\theta_0 = 0$ gives $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, yielding $\ddot{\theta} + \theta = u(t)$.

---

#### **Solution (b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

**Given:** $G(s) = 10/(s+5)$, $H(s) = 1$.

**(i) Closed-loop TF (2 marks):**

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{10}{s+5}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s+5}} = \frac{10}{s + 5 + 10} = \frac{10}{s + 15}
$$

**(ii) Steady-state error for unit step (3 marks):**

For unity feedback, the error is:

$$
E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

With $R(s) = 1/s$ (unit step):

$$
E(s) = \frac{1/s}{1 + 10/(s+5)} = \frac{1/s}{\dfrac{s+15}{s+5}} = \frac{s+5}{s(s+15)}
$$

Applying the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s\,E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s+5}{s+15} = \frac{5}{15} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333
$$

**(iii) Sensitivity (2 marks):**

$$
S_G^T = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s+5}} = \frac{s+5}{s+15}
$$

At DC ($s = 0$): $S_G^T = 5/15 = 1/3 \approx 0.333$.

**Interpretation:** A 10% variation in plant gain $G$ causes only a 3.33% variation in closed-loop gain ŌĆö a threefold reduction in sensitivity, demonstrating the **robustness provided by feedback**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall ŌĆö KTU Examiner's Note:** A common mistake is computing $e_{ss} = 1/(1+K_p)$ and using $K_p = G(0) = 2$ (i.e., $\lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = 10/5 = 2$), then saying $e_{ss} = 1/3$ ŌĆö this **is** correct, but the Full Final-Value-Theorem method shown above is required when the problem says "show the calculation." Don't skip the $E(s)$ derivation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ­¤ÜĆ

- Ō£ģ **Control system** = an arrangement to regulate output to a desired value, with or without feedback.
- Ō£ģ **Open-loop:** no feedback ŌĆö simple, cheap, but **no self-correction**.
- Ō£ģ **Closed-loop (feedback):** continuously measures output, compares with reference, and corrects.
- Ō£ģ **Closed-loop TF (general):** $T(s) = G(s) / [1 + G(s)H(s)]$ ŌĆö **"forward over one plus loop gain"**.
- Ō£ģ **Unity feedback** ($H=1$): $T(s) = G(s) / [1 + G(s)]$.
- Ō£ģ **Error signal:** $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$, where $B(s) = H(s)C(s)$.
- Ō£ģ **Effects of negative feedback:** reduces sensitivity, reduces steady-state error, attenuates disturbances, improves bandwidth ŌĆö at the cost of gain and possible instability.
- Ō£ģ **Sensitivity** $S_G^T = 1/[1+GH]$ ŌĆö measures the relative change in $T$ per relative change in $G$.
- Ō£ģ **Linearization:** retain only the first-order Taylor term about the operating point ŌĆö valid for small perturbations.
- Ō£ģ **Classifications:** Open/Closed, Linear/Non-Linear, Continuous/Discrete, Time-Invariant/Time-Varying, SISO/MIMO, Servo/Regulatory.
- Ō£ģ **Disturbance rejection** at output is scaled by $1/[1+GH]$.
- Ō£ģ **Complementary sensitivity identity:** $T(s) + S(s) = 1$ ŌĆö always true.
- Ō£ģ Always **label units and signal types** on block diagrams in the KTU exam.
- Ō£ģ For a step input in a unity feedback system, $e_{ss} = 1/[1 + K_p]$, where $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision mantra for KTU:** *"Forward over one plus loop ŌĆö that is how the closed loop."* Memorize the **four feedback effects** and the **sensitivity formula** ŌĆö these alone carry ~6 marks in any KTU module-1 question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of control systems, principles of automatic control, feedback control systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT501 - CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of control systems, principles of automatic control, feedback control systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Control System?

A **Control System** is an arrangement of physical components connected or related in such a manner as to regulate, direct, or command itself or some other system to achieve a desired output (response) from a given input (command). In the KTU 2024 framework, control systems form the backbone of modern automation, robotics, process industries, and aerospace engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):** A control system is a device, or set of devices, that manages, commands, directs, or regulates the behaviour of other devices or systems to achieve a desired objective. The **objective** is to obtain the desired output (controlled variable) by manipulating the **input** (control variable).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a speed of **60 km/h** on a highway.

- **The Driver** acts as the **Controller**. Your eyes measure the actual speed ŌåÆ compare with **60 km/h** (reference) ŌåÆ press/release the accelerator ŌåÆ engine responds ŌåÆ speed changes.
- If the car climbs a hill, speed drops ŌåÆ you press the accelerator harder (correction) ŌåÆ speed returns to 60 km/h.

This **continuous measurement ŌåÆ comparison ŌåÆ correction** cycle is precisely what a **Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control System** does, only millions of times faster and with mathematical precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Insight:** The term *Cybernetics* (coined by Norbert Wiener, 1948) literally means "the science of control and communication." Every feedback system ŌĆö from a thermostat to a Boeing 787 autopilot ŌĆö is a cybernetic system.

## 1.3 Two Foundational Categories ŌĆö The Grand Division

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Split of All Control Systems:**

| # | Type | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Open-Loop Control System** | Output is *not* measured or fed back; control action is independent of output. | Washing machine timer, toaster, traffic light (fixed cycle) |
| 2 | **Closed-Loop Control System** | Output is measured, compared with reference, and error drives the controller. | AC thermostat, missile guidance, cruise control, servo motor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A system that maintains a prescribed relationship between the output and the reference input by comparing them and using the difference (error) as a means of control is called a **feedback control system** (also called a closed-loop system).

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Control Systems?

The engineering motivations are four-fold:

1. **Performance Enhancement** ŌĆö Improve speed, accuracy, and transient response.
2. **Stability** ŌĆö Ensure the system does not oscillate or diverge.
3. **Disturbance Rejection** ŌĆö Nullify the effect of unwanted external inputs (wind gusts, load changes).
4. **Uncertainty Robustness** ŌĆö Tolerate variations in plant parameters due to ageing, temperature, or manufacturing tolerances.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Control Systems

Control systems can be classified along **multiple orthogonal axes** ŌĆö meaning a single system can belong to several categories simultaneously (e.g., a *linear, time-invariant, continuous-time, SISO, closed-loop* system).

### 2.1.1 Based on Feedback Structure

**A. Open-Loop Control Systems**

$$\boxed{\text{Open-Loop: } Y(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)}$$

The control action is generated **solely from the reference input**. There is **no measurement of the actual output**.

$$Y(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

where $G(s)$ is the forward-path transfer function.

**Characteristics of Open-Loop Systems:**
- Simple, cheap, and easy to design.
- **No disturbance rejection capability.**
- **No correction for parameter variations** ŌĆö accuracy depends entirely on calibration.
- Stable by construction (if components are stable), but **inherently inaccurate**.

**B. Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control Systems**

$$\boxed{\text{Closed-Loop: } T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}}$$

The control action depends on the **error** $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$, where $B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$.

**Characteristics of Closed-Loop Systems:**
- Reduced sensitivity to parameter variations.
- Improved disturbance rejection.
- Bandwidth extension / reduction (configurable).
- May become **unstable** if not designed properly.
- Higher cost and complexity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Mantra:** "Open-loop is *fast but blind*; Closed-loop is *accurate but risky*." A good control engineer balances both.

### 2.1.2 Based on Linearity

| Property | Linear System | Non-Linear System |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Principle of Superposition** | Holds (Additivity + Homogeneity) | Fails |
| **Mathematical model** | Linear ODE/Difference equation | Non-linear ODE (product, power, saturation) |
| **Analysis tools** | Laplace transform, Bode, Root Locus, Nyquist | Phase-plane, Lyapunov, Describing function |
| **Example** | RLC circuit, mass-spring-damper | Diode, saturation, dead-zone, relay |

**Mathematical test:** A system $f(x_1, x_2)$ is linear if and only if

$$f(a x_1 + b x_2) = a f(x_1) + b f(x_2)$$

for all scalars $a, b$ and inputs $x_1, x_2$.

### 2.1.3 Based on Time Variance

- **Time-Invariant (LTI ŌĆö Linear Time-Invariant):** Parameters do not change with time. Governed by constant-coefficient ODEs. Most textbook analysis (transfer functions) assumes LTI.
- **Time-Varying:** Parameters are functions of time, e.g., a rocket whose mass decreases as fuel burns. Modeled by time-varying coefficient ODEs.

### 2.1.4 Based on Signal Type

| Type | Signal Nature | Example |
|------|--------------|---------|
| **Continuous-Time** | Signals defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Analog circuits, motor speed control |
| **Discrete-Time** | Signals defined at discrete instants $t = kT$ | Digital computer-controlled systems, DSP |

A **Sampled-Data System** contains both continuous plant and discrete controller ŌĆö a typical industrial configuration.

### 2.1.5 Based on Number of Inputs/Outputs

- **SISO (Single-Input Single-Output):** One reference input, one controlled output. Classical control theory applies.
- **MIMO (Multi-Input Multi-Output):** Multiple inputs and outputs. Requires state-space methods, decoupling analysis.

### 2.1.6 Based on Parameter Distribution

- **Lumped-Parameter:** Elements treated as discrete; governed by ODEs.
- **Distributed-Parameter:** Parameters are spatially distributed; governed by PDEs. Example: long transmission lines, flexible space structures.

### 2.1.7 Based on Determinism

- **Deterministic:** Outputs are uniquely determined by inputs; no random noise.
- **Stochastic:** Random/stochastic inputs (noise, disturbances) are present; uses probability theory and Kalman filtering.

### 2.1.8 Based on Causal Behavior

- **Causal (Non-anticipative):** Output depends only on present and past inputs. All physical, real-time systems are causal.
- **Non-Causal:** Output depends on future inputs. Used in image processing, offline data processing.

### 2.1.9 Based on Stability Criterion (Engineering View)

- **Absolutely Stable:** Stable for *all* parameter values in the operating range.
- **Conditionally Stable:** Stable only for a *specific range* of parameter values.
- **Critically (Marginally) Stable:** Sustained oscillations (eigenvalues on imaginary axis).
- **Unstable:** Output grows without bound for any input.

### 2.1.10 Servo vs. Regulatory Systems

| Type | Objective | Reference Input | Example |
|------|-----------|----------------|---------|
| **Servo / Tracking System** | Output tracks a *time-varying* reference | $r(t)$ changes continuously | Radar antenna, CNC machine tool |
| **Regulatory / Servomechanism (constant-setpoint)** | Maintains output at a *constant* value despite disturbances | $r(t)$ is constant | Voltage regulator, room temperature controller |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap Question:** "A room temperature controller is an example of a ____ system." Answer: **Regulatory (Constant-Setpoint Servo)** system.

### 2.1.11 Static vs. Dynamic Systems

- **Static (Memoryless / Instantaneous):** Output depends only on the present input. $y(t) = f(u(t))$. Example: pure resistor $v = iR$.
- **Dynamic (with Memory):** Output depends on past, present, *and* rate of change of input. Described by differential equations. Example: RLC circuit, mass-spring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All control systems of engineering interest are DYNAMIC systems.** A static system cannot have a memory of past error ŌĆö and therefore cannot regulate intelligently.

---

## 2.2 Principles of Automatic Control ŌĆö The Engineering Foundation

### 2.2.1 Definition of Automatic Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automatic control** is the control of a system (plant/process) by a controller such that the controller operates without human intervention, using measurement of the output and pre-programmed logic to achieve the desired objective.

The word **"automatic"** is the key ŌĆö it excludes manual control, such as a human operator directly turning a valve.

### 2.2.2 The Eight Building Blocks of Any Automatic Control System

1. **Reference Input ($r$)** ŌĆö the desired value (setpoint).
2. **Comparator / Summing Junction** ŌĆö computes the error $e = r - b$.
3. **Controller ($G_c$)** ŌĆö decides corrective action from the error (P, PI, PID, etc.).
4. **Actuator ($G_a$)** ŌĆö converts controller signal into physical energy (motor, valve).
5. **Plant / Process ($G_p$)** ŌĆö the system to be controlled (motor, furnace, robot arm).
6. **Sensor / Feedback Element ($H$)** ŌĆö measures the output.
7. **Output / Controlled Variable ($c$)** ŌĆö actual achieved value.
8. **Disturbance ($D$)** ŌĆö unwanted external influence (load, wind, noise).

### 2.2.3 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Generic Form

```
                +      e      Gc     Ga     Gp        c
   r  ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║(+)ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[Gc]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[Ga]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[Gp]ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ c
                Ō¢▓       (Error)   (Controller)  (Actuator) (Plant)
                Ōöé                                       Ōöé
                Ōöé                                       Ōöé
                Ōöé         H(s)                          Ōöé
                ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ[H]ŌŚäŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
                              (Sensor)
```

The closed-loop transfer function (Mason's gain formula or block reduction):

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) G_a(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_a(s) G_p(s) H(s)}}$$

If we lump $G(s) = G_c(s) G_a(s) G_p(s)$ as the **forward path** gain:

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

### 2.2.4 The Three Pillars of Automatic Control Design

| Pillar | Goal | Key Metric |
|--------|------|------------|
| **Stability** | System must not blow up | All poles of $T(s)$ in LHP |
| **Accuracy (Steady-State)** | Final error must be small | Steady-state error $e_{ss}$, error constants $K_p, K_v, K_a$ |
| **Speed (Transient)** | Fast and well-damped response | Rise time $t_r$, Settling time $t_s$, Overshoot $M_p$ |

A good control system simultaneously optimizes all three. This is the *trade-off triangle* every KTU examiner expects you to know.

---

## 2.3 Feedback Control Systems ŌĆö The Heart of Modern Engineering

### 2.3.1 The Two Polarities of Feedback

- **Negative Feedback (desirable):** The feedback signal is **subtracted** from the reference. Reduces error, stabilizes the system, extends bandwidth. Used in **99% of practical controllers**.

$$e(t) = r(t) - b(t), \quad b(t) = H(s) c(t)$$

- **Positive Feedback (specialized):** Feedback is **added** to the reference. Causes instability / oscillation. Used in oscillators (Wien bridge, Schmitt trigger), regenerative circuits, and intentionally designed unstable modes.

### 2.3.2 The Eight Major Effects of Negative Feedback

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the **highest-weightage topics** in KTU Module 1. Memorize all eight effects.

| # | Effect | Mathematical Consequence |
|---|--------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Reduces Sensitivity to Parameter Variations** | Sensitivity $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$ |
| 2 | **Improves Disturbance Rejection** | Disturbance TF $\dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ vs open-loop $G$ |
| 3 | **Stabilizes an Unstable System** (if designed correctly) | Root-locus shifting |
| 4 | **Reduces Steady-State Error** | Error constant $K \to K(1+GH)$ |
| 5 | **Increases or Decreases System Gain** | Gain $A \to \dfrac{A}{1+AH}$ |
| 6 | **Increases Bandwidth** | $\omega_{bw,CL} = \omega_{bw,OL}(1+AH)$ (for unity feedback) |
| 7 | **Attenuates Noise (in some configurations)** | Depends on injection point |
| 8 | **May Introduce Tendency Towards Oscillation / Instability** | Risk if loop gain is too high |

### 2.3.3 Effects of Feedback ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logical Explanation

**Effect 1 ŌĆö Reduction of Sensitivity:**
For a system with open-loop gain $A$, the closed-loop gain (with unity feedback) becomes

$$A_{CL} = \frac{A}{1 + A H}$$

If $A$ changes by a small amount $\Delta A$, the corresponding change in $A_{CL}$ is

$$\Delta A_{CL} = \frac{\Delta A}{(1 + AH)^2}$$

**Sensitivity** (fractional change) is

$$S_A^{A_{CL}} = \frac{\Delta A_{CL} / A_{CL}}{\Delta A / A} = \frac{1}{1 + A H}$$

Hence, sensitivity is reduced by the factor $(1 + AH)$. Larger loop gain ŌåÆ lower sensitivity.

**Effect 2 ŌĆö Disturbance Rejection:**
With a disturbance $D(s)$ entering between the controller and plant, the output becomes

$$C(s) = \frac{G_c G}{1 + G_c G H} R(s) + \frac{G}{1 + G_c G H} D(s)$$

The disturbance term is divided by $(1 + G_c G H)$ ŌĆö same factor. **High loop gain = excellent disturbance rejection.**

**Effect 3 ŌĆö Bandwidth Extension (Unity Feedback Case):**
For a first-order plant with open-loop bandwidth $\omega_1$, closed-loop bandwidth becomes

$$\omega_{bw,CL} = \omega_1 (1 + A H)$$

The factor $(1+AH)$ extends bandwidth at the cost of increased noise and potential instability.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are tested repeatedly in KTU Board Exams and competitive exams.** They are the bedrock of Module 1.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Open-loop TF | $Y(s) = G(s)R(s)$ | No feedback |
| 2 | Closed-loop TF (single-loop) | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Unity feedback: $H=1$ |
| 3 | Error $E(s)$ | $E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s)C(s)$ | Negative feedback |
| 4 | Sensitivity of $T$ to $G$ | $S_G^T = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Within loop gain $(1+GH)$ |
| 5 | Unity feedback $T(s)$ | $T(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | Special case $H=1$ |
| 6 | Closed-loop bandwidth (1st order) | $\omega_{CL} = \omega_{OL}(1+AH)$ | Unity feedback case |
| 7 | Steady-state error (step input) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ | $K_p = \lim_{s\to 0} G(s)H(s)$ |
| 8 | Steady-state error (ramp input) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | $K_v = \lim_{s\to 0} s G(s)H(s)$ |
| 9 | Steady-state error (parabolic) | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_a}$ | $K_a = \lim_{s\to 0} s^2 G(s)H(s)$ |
| 10 | Tracking error ratio | $E/R = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | For unity feedback |
| 11 | Disturbance transfer function | $\dfrac{C}{D} = \dfrac{G_p}{1+G_c G_p H}$ | Disturbance injected at plant input |
| 12 | Characteristic equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Determines stability |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Sensitivity is *always* measured with respect to *some* parameter. The phrase "sensitivity of the system" is ambiguous ŌĆö always specify **sensitivity of output to parameter X**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Control Theory |
|-------|-------------------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Autopilot, missile guidance, satellite attitude control |
| **Robotics** | Servo motor control, walking robots, CNC machines |
| **Process Industry** | Chemical plant temperature/pressure/flow loops (PID) |
| **Automotive** | Cruise control, ABS braking, active suspension, engine ECU |
| **Power Systems** | Automatic generation control (AGC), excitation control, smart grid |
| **Biomedical** | Insulin pump, artificial heart, MRI magnetic field regulation |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Phone camera autofocus, drone flight stabilization |
| **Manufacturing** | Robotic assembly line, wafer stepper lithography |

The principles learned in Module 1 of PCRAT501 are the **conceptual bedrock** of all these systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Standard Single-Loop Feedback)

### 3.1.1 Block Diagram Setup

Consider a standard negative-feedback loop with:
- Forward path transfer function $G(s)$
- Feedback path transfer function $H(s)$
- Reference input $R(s)$
- Output $C(s)$
- Error $E(s)$
- Feedback signal $B(s)$

The signal flow is:
$$R \rightarrow (+) \rightarrow E \rightarrow [G] \rightarrow C \rightarrow [H] \rightarrow B \rightarrow \text{back to } (+)$$

### 3.1.2 Algebraic Derivation

**Step 1** ŌĆö Write the summing junction equation. In negative feedback, the error is the reference minus the feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2** ŌĆö Express the feedback signal as the output passing through $H(s)$:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 3** ŌĆö Express the output as the error passing through the forward path $G(s)$:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4** ŌĆö Substitute Step 2 into Step 1:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 5** ŌĆö Substitute this $E(s)$ into Step 3:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \big[ R(s) - H(s) C(s) \big]$$

**Step 6** ŌĆö Expand the right-hand side:

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 7** ŌĆö Collect the $C(s)$ terms on the left:

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

$$C(s) \cdot \big[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \big] = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 8** ŌĆö Solve for the closed-loop transfer function:

$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

This is the **single most important equation in control engineering** ŌĆö KTU expects derivation memorization in Semester 5.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case ŌĆö Unity Feedback ($H(s) = 1$):**
> $$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case ŌĆö Positive Feedback:**
> $$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 - G(s) H(s)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Effect of Feedback on Sensitivity

### 3.2.1 Define Sensitivity Function

The **sensitivity** of a system function $F$ to a parameter $\alpha$ is defined as:

$$S_\alpha^F = \frac{\partial F / F}{\partial \alpha / \alpha} = \frac{\alpha}{F} \cdot \frac{\partial F}{\partial \alpha}$$

Equivalently, in terms of fractional change:

$$S_\alpha^F = \frac{\Delta F / F}{\Delta \alpha / \alpha}$$

### 3.2.2 Open-Loop Case

For the open-loop system: $T_{OL} = G$. The sensitivity of $T$ to $G$ is:

$$S_G^{T_{OL}} = \frac{G}{G} \cdot \frac{\partial G}{\partial G} = 1$$

So in the open-loop case, a 10% change in $G$ produces a 10% change in $T$.

### 3.2.3 Closed-Loop Case

For the closed-loop system: $T_{CL} = \dfrac{G}{1 + GH}$.

Compute the partial derivative of $T_{CL}$ with respect to $G$:

$$\frac{\partial T_{CL}}{\partial G} = \frac{(1 + GH) \cdot 1 - G \cdot H}{(1 + GH)^2} = \frac{1 + GH - GH}{(1 + GH)^2} = \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}$$

Substitute into the sensitivity formula:

$$S_G^{T_{CL}} = \frac{G}{T_{CL}} \cdot \frac{\partial T_{CL}}{\partial G} = \frac{G(1 + GH)}{G} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + GH)^2}$$

$$\boxed{S_G^{T_{CL}} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

### 3.2.4 Interpretation

- Sensitivity is reduced by a factor of $(1 + GH)$.
- If the loop gain $GH = 100$, sensitivity = $1/101 \approx 0.0099$, i.e., less than 1% ŌĆö a **100-fold improvement** in robustness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A 10 dB increase in loop gain (factor of $\approx 3.16$) reduces sensitivity by the same factor, but at the cost of reduced phase margin and potential instability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Effect of Feedback on Disturbance Rejection

### 3.3.1 Setup

Consider a closed-loop system with a disturbance $D(s)$ entering between the controller output and the plant input:

$$C(s) = G_p(s) \cdot \big[ G_c(s) R(s) + D(s) \big]$$

The feedback equation $R(s) - H(s) C(s) = E(s)$ still applies, but now we solve for $C(s)$ in terms of *both* $R$ and $D$.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

By superposition, the output has two parts: $C(s) = C_R(s) + C_D(s)$.

**Part A ŌĆö Response to reference $R$ alone** (set $D=0$):

$$C_R(s) = \frac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H} R(s)$$

**Part B ŌĆö Response to disturbance $D$ alone** (set $R=0$):

When $R = 0$, the summing junction output is $E = -H \cdot C$, and the plant input is $G_c \cdot E + D = -G_c H C + D$. Thus:

$$C = G_p \cdot (-G_c H C + D)$$

$$C + G_c G_p H C = G_p D$$

$$C \cdot (1 + G_c G_p H) = G_p D$$

$$C_D(s) = \frac{G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) H(s)} D(s)$$

**Total output:**

$$\boxed{C(s) = \frac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H} R(s) + \frac{G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H} D(s)}$$

### 3.3.3 Conclusion

Both the reference and disturbance transfer functions share the **same denominator** $1 + G_c G_p H$, but the **disturbance numerator is just $G_p$** (without the controller in the numerator). Therefore, increasing the controller gain $G_c$ makes the disturbance term **smaller**, while keeping the reference tracking nearly unchanged (since the reference term saturates at $1/H$ as $G_c \to \infty$).

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why **High-Gain Feedback** is the engineer's secret weapon against disturbances.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Effect of Feedback on Bandwidth (First-Order Unity Feedback)

### 3.4.1 Open-Loop Transfer Function

Let the open-loop plant be a first-order system:

$$G(s) = \frac{A}{1 + s \tau}$$

Open-loop bandwidth: $\omega_{bw,OL} = 1/\tau$ (the cutoff frequency).

### 3.4.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Function

With unity feedback ($H = 1$):

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{A}{1 + s \tau}}{1 + \dfrac{A}{1 + s \tau}} = \frac{A}{1 + s \tau + A} = \frac{A}{1 + A} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{s \tau}{1 + A}}$$

### 3.4.3 Extract New Bandwidth

The closed-loop time constant is $\tau_{CL} = \dfrac{\tau}{1 + A}$.

Hence the closed-loop bandwidth is:

$$\omega_{bw,CL} = \frac{1}{\tau_{CL}} = \frac{1 + A}{\tau} = (1 + A) \cdot \omega_{bw,OL}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{bw,CL} = (1 + A) \omega_{bw,OL}}$$

### 3.4.4 Interpretation

The bandwidth is increased by the factor $(1 + A)$. The system now responds faster but is more sensitive to high-frequency noise.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Tracking Error of Unity Feedback System

### 3.5.1 Error Equation

For unity feedback, $E(s) = R(s) - C(s)$. Also $C(s) = G(s) E(s)$. Hence:

$$E(s) = R(s) - G(s) E(s)$$

$$E(s) \cdot (1 + G(s)) = R(s)$$

$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \quad \text{(general)}}$$

### 3.5.2 Steady-State Error via Final Value Theorem

Assuming the closed-loop system is stable:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

**For unit step input** $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p}$$

where $\boxed{K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)}$ is the **position error constant**.

**For unit ramp input** $R(s) = 1/s^2$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s^2}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{\lim_{s \to 0} s G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{K_v}$$

where $\boxed{K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s G(s)H(s)}$ is the **velocity error constant**.

**For unit parabolic input** $R(s) = 1/s^3$:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{\lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{K_a}$$

where $\boxed{K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G(s)H(s)}$ is the **acceleration error constant**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Quick-Reference Comparison Table

| Property | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Feedback** | Absent | Present (negative) |
| **Sensitivity** | High (unity) | Reduced by $(1+GH)$ |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | None | Excellent |
| **Steady-State Accuracy** | Poor | High |
| **Bandwidth** | Fixed | Adjustable (often extended) |
| **Stability** | Inherently stable if components stable | Conditional; can become unstable |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Low | High |
| **Transient Response** | Uncontrolled | Tunable via controller |
| **Noise Susceptibility** | Low (no amplification) | Higher (due to high gain) |
| **Linearity of Overall TF** | Depends on $G$ | Linearized despite nonlinear plant (over operating range) |
| **Example** | Traffic light | Servo positioner |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams are used to convey the *block-level functional architecture* of control systems. They are KTU-acceptable schematics and align with the official syllabus diagrams.

## 4.1 Diagram 1 ŌĆö Open-Loop Control System Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR["Reference Input r"]
    nodeC["Controller Gc"]
    nodeA["Actuator Ga"]
    nodeP["Plant Gp"]
    nodeY["Output c"]
    nodeD["Disturbance d"]

    nodeR --> nodeC --> nodeA --> nodeP --> nodeY

    nodeD -.-> nodeP

    classDef def fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    class nodeR,nodeC,nodeA,nodeP,nodeY,nodeD def
```

**Interpretation:** The reference $r$ flows through Controller, Actuator, and Plant in one direction. Disturbance $d$ enters the plant *uncompensated* ŌĆö the output is unaware of it.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 ŌĆö Closed-Loop (Negative Feedback) Control System

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR["Reference r"]
    nodeSum(("+ Sum"))
    nodeE["Error e = r - b"]
    nodeGc["Controller Gc"]
    nodeGa["Actuator Ga"]
    nodeP["Plant Gp"]
    nodeY["Output c"]
    nodeH["Sensor H"]
    nodeB["Feedback b"]
    nodeD["Disturbance d"]

    nodeR --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeE --> nodeGc --> nodeGa --> nodeP --> nodeY
    nodeY --> nodeH --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeSum

    nodeD -.-> nodeP

    classDef def fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45F06,color:#000
    class nodeR,nodeSum,nodeE,nodeGc,nodeGa,nodeP,nodeY,nodeH,nodeB,nodeD def
```

**Interpretation:** The error $e$ drives the controller; the output is measured by $H$, fed back to the summing junction, and subtracted from the reference.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 ŌĆö Signal Flow Graph of a Feedback System (Mason's Format)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR["R(s)"]
    nodeE["E(s)"]
    nodeC["C(s)"]
    nodeY["Y(s)"]

    nodeR -->|"1"| nodeE
    nodeE -->|"G(s)"| nodeC
    nodeC -->|"1"| nodeY
    nodeY -->|"-H(s)"| nodeE

    classDef def fill:#EAF7E1,stroke:#38761D,color:#000
    class nodeR,nodeE,nodeC,nodeY def
```

**Interpretation:** This is a 3-node signal flow graph. The forward-path gain is $G(s)$, the loop gain is $-G(s)H(s)$, and the closed-loop transfer function (Mason's rule) is:

$$T(s) = \frac{\sum P_k \Delta_k}{\Delta} = \frac{G(s) \cdot 1}{1 - (-G(s)H(s))} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

## 4.4 Diagram 4 ŌĆö Functional Architecture of an Industrial PID Control Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSP["Setpoint r"]
    nodeComp(("Compare"))
    nodeErr["Error e"]
    nodePID["PID Controller Gc"]
    nodeValve["Control Valve / Actuator"]
    nodePlant["Industrial Plant Furnace/Reactor"]
    nodeOut["Process Variable c"]
    nodeTrans["Temperature Transmitter H"]
    nodeFB["Feedback b"]
    nodeNoise["Load Disturbance d"]

    nodeSP --> nodeComp
    nodeComp --> nodeErr --> nodePID --> nodeValve --> nodePlant --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeTrans --> nodeFB --> nodeComp

    nodeNoise -.-> nodePlant

    classDef def fill:#F4E6F4,stroke:#741B47,color:#000
    class nodeSP,nodeComp,nodeErr,nodePID,nodeValve,nodePlant,nodeOut,nodeTrans,nodeFB,nodeNoise def
```

**Interpretation:** A practical representation of a temperature/pressure/flow control loop. The **setpoint** $r$ is compared with the **measured** value $b$; the **PID controller** generates a corrective signal; the **valve** manipulates the flow; the **transmitter** senses the output.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 ŌĆö Classification Tree of Control Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Control Systems"]
    A --> B["Based on Feedback"]
    A --> C["Based on Linearity"]
    A --> D["Based on Time"]
    A --> E["Based on Signal"]
    A --> F["Based on I/O Count"]
    A --> G["Based on Stability"]
    A --> H["Based on Application"]

    B --> B1["Open-Loop"]
    B --> B2["Closed-Loop"]

    C --> C1["Linear"]
    C --> C2["Non-Linear"]

    D --> D1["Time-Invariant"]
    D --> D2["Time-Varying"]

    E --> E1["Continuous-Time"]
    E --> E2["Discrete-Time"]
    E --> E3["Sampled-Data"]

    F --> F1["SISO"]
    F --> F2["MIMO"]

    G --> G1["Stable"]
    G --> G2["Unstable"]
    G --> G3["Marginally Stable"]

    H --> H1["Servo / Tracking"]
    H --> H2["Regulatory"]

    classDef def fill:#D9E8F5,stroke:#0B5394,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,B1,B2,C1,C2,D1,D2,E1,E2,E3,F1,F2,G1,G2,G3,H1,H2 def
```

**Interpretation:** The complete taxonomy of control systems discussed in KTU Module 1. Each axis is **independent** ŌĆö a single physical system can fall into multiple categories (e.g., *linear, time-invariant, SISO, continuous-time, closed-loop, servo* ŌĆö a typical DC motor position control).
